name: Data Engineering
description: Data pipelines, ETL, warehousing, and streaming systems
axioms: ETL vs ELT: extract-transform-load vs extract-load-transform (modern = ELT)
Schema evolution: schemas change; pipelines must handle backward/forward compatibility
Idempotency: running pipeline twice produces same result as running once
Data quality: completeness, accuracy, consistency, timeliness, validity
Partitioning: split data by time/key for parallel access and lifecycle management
Exactly-once semantics is hard; at-least-once + idempotency is practical
reasoning_patterns: Data modeling: star schema, snowflake, data vault for warehousing
Pipeline design: batch vs streaming vs micro-batch (choose by latency requirements)
Data quality checks: schema validation, null checks, range checks, anomaly detection
Backfill strategy: how to reprocess historical data without breaking downstream
Data lineage: track where data came from and how it was transformed
boundary_conditions: Does not cover data science/analysis in depth (see 633)
Does not cover specific cloud services (AWS, GCP, Azure) in detail
Data governance and privacy (GDPR) require legal expertise
key_references: Kimball & Ross. The Data Warehouse Toolkit
Housley, M. Data Engineering with Python
canonical_truths: Data pipelines break. Design for failure, not success.
Schema-on-read vs schema-on-write is a fundamental architectural choice
Partitioning by date is almost always the right first partition
CDC (Change Data Capture) is the modern way to sync databases
Data lakes become data swamps without governance and cataloging
